title: Using a heat gun to strip furniture paint
subtitle: Soften and scrape multiple coats of paint from timber furniture
excerpt: How to use a heat gun to strip old paint from timber furniture: setting the correct temperature for the substrate, working in sections to keep the paint at the peel point, scraping cleanly without scorching, and preparing the surface for refinishing.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.
aliases: paint removal with hot air gun

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a paint layer softens enough to be lifted cleanly with a scraper without needing pressure. At the peel point, the paint wrinkles and lifts in sheets rather than crumbling. Too low and the scraper drags; too high and the timber scorches.

A paint containing lead oxide pigment, commonly used on furniture before the 1960s. Heating lead paint releases lead fumes; sanding it produces lead dust. On furniture suspected of being pre-1960s, use a lead test kit before applying heat or sanding. If lead is present, use chemical stripper rather than a heat gun.

content.text: Before using a heat gun on old furniture, test for
lead paint

if the piece is likely to be pre-1960. Use a lead test swab (available from DIY stores) on a small area. If the test is positive, switch to a chemical stripper. For post-1960 furniture, a heat gun reaches the
peel point
faster than any other method and does not raise the grain.
Step 1, prepare the workspace

Work outdoors or in a well-ventilated space; heated paint gives off fumes regardless of whether lead is present. Wear safety glasses: softened paint can flick from the scraper. Keep a heatproof surface or metal tray nearby to receive the hot scrapings. Clear the area of combustible materials; the heat gun nozzle surface temperature is 300 to 500 degrees Celsius.
Step 2, set the temperature and test

Set the heat gun to its lower setting (around 300 to 350 degrees) for thin softwood and veneered surfaces, and to the medium setting (around 450 degrees) for thick gloss on solid hardwood. Test on a small area: hold the nozzle 25 to 50 mm (1 to 2 in) from the surface, keep the gun moving, and test with the scraper after 10 to 15 seconds. The paint should lift cleanly and roll off the scraper. If it smears and pulls, the temperature is too low. If the timber beneath is scorching, the temperature is too high or the gun is too close.
Step 3, work in sections

Heat a section of 100 to 150 mm (4 to 6 in) at a time, keeping the gun moving in a slow pass. Follow immediately behind with the scraper held at a low angle (30 to 40 degrees). The paint must be scraped while it is still hot and soft; if it cools before the scraper arrives it reharddens and must be re-heated. On moulded profiles and corners, use a small filling knife rather than the wide scraper.
Step 4, remove residue and sand back

After the main paint is removed, sand the surface with 80-grit to remove any remaining paint in the grain, working with the grain not across it. Follow with 120-grit to remove the scratches from the 80-grit. On softwood, apply a wood filler to any heat-raised grain before sanding. The surface is ready for primer or a finish when it is clean, smooth, and free of residue.
Common mistakes
Holding the gun in one place.
A stationary heat gun scorches and chars the timber within 5 seconds. Always keep it moving in a slow, steady pass.
Using a heat gun on MDF or plywood veneer.
The adhesive in MDF and plywood releases at heat gun temperatures, causing delamination and bubbling. Use chemical stripper on composite boards.
Laying the gun down hot without a rest stand.
The nozzle remains hot for several minutes. Always rest the gun on a heatproof surface or use the built-in stand, never lay it on its side on the bench.
